claims.text: The European Great Famine of 1315–1317 triggered one of the worst population collapses in European history and ranks as the single worst European famine in mortality as a proportion of population (Baek et al. 2020, Communications Earth & Environment).

Historical records point to torrential rainfall, land saturation, crop failure, and prolonged flooding as important causes of the famine; tree-ring reconstruction (Old World Drought Atlas) shows the average of the growing seasons preceding the Great Famine years (1314–1316) was the fifth wettest over Europe from 1300 to 2012 CE (Baek et al. 2020).

A peer-reviewed study of selective mortality in medieval London describes famine mortality levels that 'might have been as high as 10–15%' as being 'on par with the later Great Famine c. 1315–1317 CE', placing Great Famine mortality on the order of 10–15% of the population in England.

A peer-reviewed modeling study states that 'one of the worst population collapses of human societies occurred during the early fourteenth century in northern Europe', attributing the Great Famine to climate deterioration during the transition from the Medieval Warm Period to the Little Ice Age.

description: The Great Famine of 1315–1317 struck northern Europe when torrential rainfall, land saturation, and prolonged flooding caused successive harvest failures beginning in 1315. It triggered one of the worst population collapses in European history and ranks as the single worst European famine in mortality as a proportion of population. Some scholars extend the agrarian crisis, which included a devastating livestock pestilence, to 1322.

impact.summary: Absolute death tolls are not reliably quantified — verified scholarly sources state mortality proportionally, not in absolute numbers. A peer-reviewed study of medieval London mortality puts Great Famine mortality on the order of 10–15% of the population, and Baek et al. (2020) rank it as the single worst European famine in mortality as a proportion of population. Converting these shares to absolute deaths would require medieval population baselines the verified sources do not state, so the bounds are left null.

location.country: Northern Europe (modern UK, Ireland, France, Belgium, Netherlands, Germany, and neighbors)
location.region: From the British Isles across the northern European plain; the crisis in England is the best documented

name: Great Famine of 1315–1317
